19 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How I built an AI assistant that knows the lesson recordings, code files, notebooks, and technical notes from a four-month training program — and what the process taught me about building useful AI systems
There is one sentence my instructor said in class that has stayed with me for a long time:
“If you do not use artificial intelligence correctly, it is like a swing. It keeps moving, but it does not take you anywhere.”
At first, I thought this was simply a warning about using AI tools in the wrong way.
Later, I realized that it was actually a much deeper engineering lesson.
Today, connecting to a powerful language model is easy.
Building a chat interface is easy.
Uploading a few documents to a vector database and creating a working demo is also relatively easy.
The difficult part is something else:

• Clearly defining the real problem

• Measuring whether the system is retrieving the correct information

• Understanding the difference between a fluent answer and a correct answer

• Identifying whether a failure comes from the model, the data, the retrieval layer, or the prompt

• Moving the system from a local computer to a real usage environment

• Knowing when to increase architectural complexity and when to keep the system intentionally simple
This project started exactly at the intersection of these questions.
The AI Agents course by Google and Kaggle, together with the agent architecture and RAG lessons in my own bootcamp, helped one idea become clear in my mind:
If an AI system can connect to a specific knowledge source, build context from that source, and guide the user based on a particular problem, why should my own training archive not become that knowledge source?
During our four-month training program, we created a large amount of material:

• Lesson recordings

• Python files

• Jupyter notebooks

• PDF documents

• Code examples

• Technical explanations

• Project notes
The knowledge was there.
But it was scattered.
It was not easy to search.
Whenever I started a new project, it took time to remember which topic we had covered, which notebook contained the relevant method, and which approach would be the best starting point.
So I decided to build an AI assistant that could work with the full training archive of my instructor.
My goal was not to create a chatbot that simply searched through files.
I wanted a system that could review a new project and help me answer questions such as:

• What type of problem is this?

• In which lesson did we study a similar approach?

• Which method should I start with?

• Why is that method suitable?

• Which alternatives should I also consider?

• What are the weaknesses of the selected approach?

• Which risks should I check in the data first?

• Is the available information enough to make a decision?
Today, the system is a working application that I can access from my phone.
But the application itself was not the most valuable outcome.
The most valuable part was everything I learned while building it.
Because in RAG projects, the biggest challenge is usually not the language model.
The real challenge is everything that happens before and after the model.
Why I Call It a “Digital Twin”
I describe the project as my instructor’s “digital twin.”
However, it is important to define the limits of that expression.
In industrial systems, a digital twin is usually a digital representation of a physical machine, process, system, or organization. It is often updated with real-time data.
My system does not fully reproduce my instructor’s personality, experience, judgment, or decision-making process.
So, in a strict technical sense, it is not a complete digital twin.
A more accurate definition would be:
A RAG-based knowledge and project guidance assistant built on my instructor’s training materials.
I use the term “digital twin” as a metaphor.
The system makes the knowledge shared by my instructor during the training program available in a digital and searchable form. It allows me to return to that knowledge whenever I need guidance.
This distinction matters.
A system that knows an expert’s materials is not the same as a system that has learned how that expert thinks.
My current system achieves the first.
The second would require different data, stronger evaluation methods, and a more advanced architecture.
The Core Architecture
The system is built around RAG.
RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
Instead of asking the language model to answer only from its general training knowledge, the system first searches an external knowledge base. It then uses the retrieved material as context while generating the answer.
The process has three main stages.
1. Preparing and Indexing the Knowledge
I first collected the available training materials:

• Jupyter notebooks

• Python source files

• PDF documents

• Word documents

• CSV and Excel files

• Text notes

• Lesson content

• Project descriptions
Sending all of these files directly to a language model would not be practical or efficient.
So I first extracted the content and divided it into smaller pieces.
Each piece was converted into a high-dimensional vector using an embedding model and then stored in ChromaDB.
At this stage, it was important to preserve not only the content but also the source of that content.
I added the source path at the beginning of each chunk:
[SOURCE: TIME_SERIES/TimeSeriesAnalysis.ipynb]This allowed folder names, course modules, and file names to become part of the search signal.
2. Retrieval
When the user asks a question, the question is also converted into a vector using the same embedding model.
The system then searches ChromaDB for the most semantically similar content.
For example, when a user asks:
“I have date, region, product, and sales quantity data. Which forecasting approach should I start with?”
The system does not immediately generate a general answer.
It first searches the training archive for related content about:

• Time series

• Regression

• Feature engineering

• Seasonality

• Forecast evaluation
3. Answer Generation
The retrieved content is then sent to the language model as context.
But simply telling the model to “answer the question” is not enough.
I ask the model to follow a more disciplined process:

• Identify the problem type.

• Find the relevant training material.

• Explain the suggested method.

• Justify why that method was selected.

• Compare alternative approaches.

• Explain risks and limitations.

• Mention the source files.

• Clearly say when there is not enough information in the training archive.
This is where the real difference appears.
A general chatbot may give you general information about time series forecasting.
A source-based assistant should be able to produce an answer such as:
This problem is similar to the time-series methods covered in the training materials. However, before choosing ARIMA or LSTM, trend, seasonality, stationarity, and data frequency should be checked. If there are many products and regions, a global forecasting model may be more suitable than building a separate model for each series.
For me, the value of the system is not only in naming a method.
The real value is in explaining why that method may be appropriate.
The Technology Stack
The working version of the project uses:

• Python for data processing, indexing, and application logic

• ChromaDB as the persistent vector database

• Sentence-Transformers for creating embeddings

• multilingual-e5-base for Turkish and multilingual semantic search

• OpenRouter for accessing language models

• A Gemini-based model for answer generation

• Streamlit for the user interface

• GitHub for version control

• Streamlit Cloud for deployment
Listing technologies is easy.
But the quality of the system does not come from using popular tools.
The real quality depends on decisions such as:

• Which content should be indexed?

• Which content should be excluded?

• How should chunks be created?

• Is the embedding model suitable for the language of the data?

• Are the retrieved results actually relevant?

• Does the model stay grounded in the source material?

• How are local and cloud environments managed?

• How are uploaded user files processed?

• How are the system’s limits defined?
The Biggest Misunderstanding About RAG
RAG systems are often explained in a very simple way:

• Upload documents.

• Split them into chunks.

• Store them in a vector database.

• Ask questions.

• Generate answers.
This flow is technically correct.
But it is not enough for a reliable RAG system.
A real system must answer questions such as:

• Which files should be indexed?

• Which content should be excluded?

• Should code and explanatory text be chunked in the same way?

• How should chunk size be selected?

• Should headings and source information be preserved?

• How many chunks should be retrieved for each question?

• Should low-similarity results be removed?

• How should repeated results from the same file be handled?

• How should retrieval quality be measured?

• Is the generated answer really supported by the source?

• How can we detect when the model adds unsupported information?

• How should uploaded user files be separated from the permanent knowledge base?
One of the clearest lessons from this project was:
RAG is mainly a retrieval engineering problem.
The language model is the most visible part of the system.
But retrieval often determines whether the final answer will be useful or misleading.
Challenges I Faced
1. A Working System Is Not Necessarily a Correct System
In the first versions, the system did not produce errors.
I asked a question.
It returned an answer.
The interface worked.
This created the feeling that the system was successful.
Later, I realized that the model sometimes used a document that was only loosely related to the question instead of retrieving the most relevant source.
This is one of the most dangerous types of failure in AI systems:
Silent failure.
The system does not crash.
It does not show an error message.
It produces a fluent and convincing answer.
But it uses the wrong context.
In traditional software, errors are often visible.
In LLM-based systems, errors can hide inside well-written paragraphs.
For this reason, evaluating only the final answer is not enough.
The full chain must be inspected:

• Was the query interpreted correctly?

• Was the correct chunk retrieved?

• Was the retrieved content sufficient?

• Did the model use the content correctly?

• Did the model add unsupported information?
2. Using the Wrong Embedding Model for Turkish Content
In the first version, I used an embedding approach that was more suitable for English content.
The system still worked.
But Turkish questions and Turkish materials were not matched strongly enough, so the correct content did not always appear among the top results.
I later switched to a multilingual embedding model.
This improved not only the final answers but also the reliability of the retrieval layer.
The lesson was clear:
The embedding model is not a small technical detail. It is one of the main architectural decisions of the knowledge retrieval system.
A popular model is not automatically the right model for your data.
It is also not enough to choose a model based only on its name or benchmark results.
You need a small evaluation set based on real questions.
For example, you can prepare 30–50 questions and test:

• Does the expected source appear in the top three results?

• Does the result change when the same question is asked in a different way?

• What happens when Turkish and English technical terms are used together?

• Can the system find content by file name?

• Can conceptual questions reach the correct notebook?
Embedding quality should be evaluated with a test set, not only by intuition.
3. Most of My Knowledge Base Was Not Really Knowledge
After the first indexing proces
